utterances: There was something to it.
I don't want to suggest that politics wasn't a contact sport.

But I can tell you that, you know, on the Senate Finance Committee, which was a very important committee, dealing with taxation and the anti-entitlement programs, everything was done on a bipartisan basis.
Nothing could succeed without both parties being together on it.
So there was a different feel to it than, I think, exists today.
There really has been a breakdown in any sense of bipartisanship and a breakdown in any sense of comity between the two parties.
That doesn't bode well for getting something done.
How do you think either side will move the ball down the field?
Well, I think that a lot, now, depends on the president.
I mean the president did win the election.
The president is in charge.
And the question now is, well, what does the president want to do?
It's not that the Republicans don't have a responsibility to try to reach some sort of agreement, of course they do.
But I think that the lead in this is the president.
Thank you very much for joining us.
Thank you.
John Danforth is a former Republican senator from Missouri and also served as U.S.
ambassador to the United Nations.
(SOUNDBITE OF MUSIC)
It's MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
This is MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Renee Montagne.
And I'm David Greene.
Mitt Romney chalked up three more high-profile endorsements this week from opposite ends of the Republican ideological spectrum.
This does add fuel to Romney's effort to paint himself as his party's inevitable candidate - ahead of next week's primaries in Wisconsin, Maryland and Washington, D.C.
NPR's Wade Goodwyn has that report.
There's been growing recognition inside the Republican establishment that the primary is not doing great things for Mitt Romney's image.
A CNN poll earlier this week had only 37 percent of those polled now with a favorable view of the former Massachusetts governor.
With an eye to building some momentum, yesterday former President George H.
W.
Bush, that's the first President Bush, endorsed Romney in his office in Houston.
Well, I think it's time for the people to all get behind this good man.
We're so convinced, and maybe because we've known Mitt for a long time, that he's the man to do this job and get on and win the presidency.
It was a nice moment for Governor Romney, who was clearly appreciative.
Having your support means a great deal to me on a personal basis, a family basis, and also on a national basis.
The former president's wife, Barbara, has already been working for Romney, recording robocalls for his campaign.
And his son, former Florida Governor Jeb Bush, has also already endorsed Romney.
And Wednesday night, freshman Florida senator Marco Rubio came out for the frontrunner.
Rubio expressed concern that if the race continued until the Republican convention, the eventual nominee would be too badly damaged.
And I think that a floor fight in Tampa would be the worst possible thing we can do in terms of winning in November.
And this morning, Romney added one more conservative voice to his roster of supporters â House Budget Committee Chairman Paul Ryan.
Wade Goodwyn, NPR News, Dallas.
And in South Africa, unions are trying to block Wal-Mart from taking over a local retailer.
Earlier this year, the South Africa's government approved Wal-Mart's bid to buy Massmart.
Now it's reconsidering that ruling.
From Cape Town, Anders Kelto sent this report.
(SOUNDBITE OF SINGING)
Outside the Cape Town High court, a large group of protesters gathers on the sidewalk.
They hold red and white signs and wear T-shirts that say: The Anti-Wal-Mart Coalition.
And, because this is a South African protest, they sing.
(SOUNDBITE OF SINGING AND CLAPPING)
Jamil Jacobs has worked at a grocery store here in Cape Town for the past 25 years.
Well, we are here to protest against Wal-Mart.
Wal-Mart has agreed to purchase Massmart for more than $2 billion.
They're hoping to use the retail chain, which operates in 14 African countries, to reach Africa's rapidly growing consumer markets.
But Mike Abrahams, a spokesperson for the South African Commercial, Catering and Allied Workers Union, says unions are opposed to the merger.
Now, we know from our experience and international relations that even in the U.S., workers are not allowed to join unions within Wal-Mart.
We know from our experience internationally that where they have moved, they systematically eroded workers' rights and things like that.
We don't want them here.
Dr. James Maguire is the director of the International Health Division at the University of Maryland.
He worked in a rural area in Bolivia where at one time Chagas had been detected in 45 percent of the people.
He describes it as a disease of the poor, whose mud and stick homes with patched roofs provide a place for the insects to hide.
Children bitten that might often don't experience symptoms until decades later as adults.
People become short of breath.
They develop edema in the lower extremities.
They become weak.
And the average survival after the first signs of congestive heart failure without intervention is very short.
A couple of years in over half of the people will have died from congestive heart failure.
It is treatable if diagnosed before chronic conditions develop.
(Through translator) No. I've never heard of it.
I didn't know anything about this disease.
Raul Flores has never heard of Chagas disease nor had any of the other patients of La Clinica Del Pueblo in Washington, D.C., which serves thousands of Latin American immigrants.
Clinical director Dr. Meredith Josephs says she's seen one patient who'd already been diagnosed by the time she began treating him for congestive heart failure.
She says her patients are worried just as much about how they will be perceived.
I think it would be important to recognize that this parasite is not transmitted from person=to-person contact.
So that other persons that do carry this parasite are not putting other people at risk of the disease.
I would hate to see for there to be widespread fear with this new information coming out.
Some experts say that people who believe they've been exposed to the parasite may want to be tested for Trypanosoma cruzi, the parasite that causes Chagas disease.
But neither the World Health Organization nor the Centers of Disease Control and Prevention recommend widespread screening for the disease.
Brenda Wilson, NPR News.
This is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
I'm Robert Siegel.
And I'm Michele Norris.
A new survey finds a big shift in the political attitudes of people in this country, and a reversal of fortunes for the major parties.

The Pew Research Center for the People & the Press has been tracking Americans' opinions and values since 1987, and the center's director, Andrew Kohut, is with us to talk about the changes it's now picking up.
Welcome, Andy.
Glad you're here.
Happy to be here.
Now, let's start with the broader issues.
What's going on?
Well, we see more support for the social safety net.
We have about four questions that we've been asking since 1987 that are all going in the same direction.
Should the government take care of those who can't take care of themselves?
Only 57 percent agreed with that statement in 1994.
It's climbed up to 69 percent now.
But it's inched up over the '90s.
What we see is a reversal of the movement toward conservative attitudes, toward the social safety net that occurred in the mid-'90s, changing as we reach 2007.
More people saying the government should guarantee that everyone has enough money for food and shelter.
I wonder if Katrina has anything to do with it.
You know, I was thinking that myself.

These numbers have been going in this direction, but now in this survey, the last one was done in '03, this one done in '06 - late '06, early '07 - the intervening event may have been Katrina showing the underside of life in America.
And it's clearly there.
We also see more concern about income inequality, and we see a big partisan gap in the way people look at their own finances.
When we first started doing these surveys, Republicans, Democrats and independents all made the same judgments about how well they were doing financially.
In this survey, 81 percent of Republicans said they were doing well.
Only 50 percent of independents and Democrats said they're doing financially well.
So how do these changes translate to attitudes not just about people themselves but attitudes about the political parties?
Well, we do see Democrats really opening up a bigger advantage.
Four years ago, Democrats had a small advantage when you consider the number of people who say they're either Democrat or independent leaning Democrat.
It was 47-41.
It's now opened up to a wide 50 to 35 percent gap, reflecting more people saying that - somewhat more people saying they're Democrats and somewhat more independents saying they lean Democratic.
